title: Understanding Concepts
excerpt:
The Secret to Better Coding

When you're learning to code, the "concept" is the most important thing to grasp. You can think of it as the core logic or the formula...
markdown:
The Secret to Better Coding

When you're learning to code, the "concept" is the most important thing to grasp. You can think of it as the core logic or the formula behind a feature.

To break it down, here is a simple example: **a + b = x**

To get the result **x**, you have to understand that you need **a** and **b** first. That's the concept—knowing exactly what you need to achieve your goal before you even touch the keyboard.

### Why does this matter?

Here are three reasons why focusing on the concept is a game-changer:

1. **You know your end goal:** You aren't just typing random code; you have a clear vision of what you are building.

2. **You know your dependencies:** Just like in the math example, you identify exactly what inputs or data you need to reach the result.

3. **You have intentionality:** This is the most important part—you actually understand *what* you are doing and *why* you are doing it, rather than just copying and pasting from tutorials.

### The Bottom Line

By mastering the concept first, your coding and problem-solving skills will become so much stronger. It's easy to get lost in syntax, but if you remember to **"Understand the Concept,"** everything else will fall into place naturally.

title: What is an API?
excerpt:
Hi everyone! Sorry for the radio silence, lately it's been a busy few weeks. Let’s dive straight back into it with our next post.

**Let’s get straight to the point:** An **API (Application Program...
markdown:
Hi everyone! Sorry for the radio silence, lately it's been a busy few weeks. Let’s dive straight back into it with our next post.

**Let’s get straight to the point:** An **API (Application Programming Interface)** is simply a way for different apps to talk to each other. No hidden tricks, no fluff—just a direct line of communication.

### The Real-World Example
I bet you’ve seen pages that allow you to "Login with Google" or "Login with Facebook." How do they do it? You guessed it: **APIs.**

Imagine we have **Application A** and **Application B**:
1. **A** wants to get some info from **B**, but they are separate companies.
2. **B** is willing to share that data, provided certain terms and conditions are met.
3. **B** prepares an API and writes documentation on how to use it.
4. **A** follows those instructions to make the connection. 


### What does an API actually look like?
In many cases, an API looks like a URL (often called an *endpoint*). For example:
`https://api.example.com/v1/users`

But it’s rarely that easy to access. If an API were completely open, anyone could use (or abuse) the data. To keep things safe, developers use **Authentication**. They will provide you with a unique **Token** or "Key" to prove you have permission to be there. 

**Pro-tip:** Always refer to the documentation to get a clear picture of the security requirements.

### Conclusion
An API is a gateway that provides information through a structured, secure connection. While we often use them to connect different companies, they are also used within the same application to help different parts of the system stay organized.

**Remember:** An API is just another way of communicating. Hope this clears things up!

***

*Please leave a comment if you have any questions or if there's a specific tech topic you'd like me to cover next!*

title: How Important Reading is
excerpt:
# The Underrated Superpower: The Art of Reading

In the world of programming, what does "reading" actually mean? It means more than just glancing at words. Reading error messages and documentation is vital. It is the fuel that allows...
markdown:
# The Underrated Superpower: The Art of Reading

In the world of programming, what does "reading" actually mean? It means more than just glancing at words. Reading error messages and documentation is vital. It is the fuel that allows us to move forward quickly when we hit a wall. Reading isn’t just a task; it’s a time-saving strategy that allows you to react and solve problems with speed.

### Read to the Very End

One of the most important habits you can build is reading to the end of a document or a StackOverflow answer. Why? Because the solution is often in the context. Understanding the end result *before* you start typing helps you avoid wrong turns.

**If you don’t understand what you’re reading:**

* **Read it in "slow-mo":** Break down the sentences word by word.
* **Step away:** If you’re still stuck, take a break. Have a snack, get some fresh air, and come back to it.
* **Try again:** Read it slowly once more. Trust me, it works every time.

### A Habit with Life-Changing Side Effects

Developing a habit of technical reading carries over into your daily life. You’ll find yourself more attentive when buying products, signing up for services, or reviewing contracts.

However, remember this simple logic:

* **Reading without understanding = 0**
* **Reading + Understanding = 1**

When you truly understand, you are at the "true gate," and you can finally move forward with confidence.

### Conclusion

Read until the end. Read to understand. Take breaks when needed. This practice will lift you up, save you hours of debugging, and ultimately make you a much better team player.

So, cheer up and start reading before you start implementing!

title: Git
excerpt: Git is an awesome tool that is used by programmers all around the world. It was an amazing creation by the founder of Linux. It is a time machine that can...
markdown:
Git is an awesome tool that is used by programmers all around the world. It was an amazing creation by the founder of Linux. It is a time machine that can bring back any of your code from the past with just a few commands.

How do programmers work together in the real world? Don’t say they transfer their code via thumb drives?? definitely not, right? Git is the place where they host their code and collaborate with their colleagues.

You can install Git on your own server, or you can use services like www.github.com, www.bitbucket.org, and others. By now, I hope you guys understand what Git is. Now, let’s go to the next step.

### Repository (Repo)
'Repository,' or 'repo' for short, is equivalent to a folder—a folder that stores your source code. You can have multiple repos. Each repo gets its own URL, which you can share with your colleagues to start collaborating. That’s all about repos for this post. Explore it yourself and ask me if you have any questions!

### GitHub and Bitbucket
These two are cloud services that handle Git for you. As mentioned in my previous post, go ahead and search or read up about GitHub and Bitbucket. In this post, I will just give you guys a few hints.

**Bitbucket:** It's great for private repos and allows up to 5 people to collaborate for free. For more details, you guys can check out the pricing section on their site. What I really want to mention is the UX, in my personal opinion, Bitbucket's UX is so neat and really easy to use. You guys should check it out.


**GitHub:** They give free repos too, but with some limitations (check it out on their site). GitHub is really good for hosting static websites (e.g., https://skolacode.github.io/). GitHub's main intention is to help you share your knowledge with others, so they prefer you to make your repos public. It is used by developers around the globe to share their code.

Soon, I will publish more posts about Git features (conflicts, tags, rollbacks, etc.). Stay tuned guys!

title: Hash and Encrypt
excerpt:
Guys, today’s topic is simple but quite interesting: What is HASH and ENCRYPT, and when should you use them? This will be my sharing for today.

First, what is HASH...
markdown:
Guys, today’s topic is simple but quite interesting: What is HASH and ENCRYPT, and when should you use them? This will be my sharing for today.

## HASH
First, what is **HASH**? Hashing is a method that we use to turn our text into gibberish. By doing this, NO ONE can decode it. Yes, you heard it right—there is no reversing it. That is what makes it safe and secure.

### Example:

Test 1:
Hello world > hashed > $2y$10$zWQP1I6/vZF4QrDBkDaC…

Test 2:
Hello world > hashed > $2y$10$.JbZIG53ewCTglqpuPTjP.…

Above is the result of the exact same text, with no difference at all, but we get completely different results. Awesome, right! So, when should we use HASH? We must use HASH when storing a password. It is very important to store passwords via HASH to keep our system secure. If you haven't hashed them yet, please fix it ASAP, guys. There is a witch that casts magic at our backend, so you don't even need to worry about the algorithm.
## ENCRYPT
Next, let’s move to Encrypt. Encryption is awesome too! These two (HASH & Encrypt) are not rivals; they are meant for different purposes, so don't confuse them. When should we use Encrypt? Encryption is used in secure communications. Both parties will have either a public or private key so they can understand what they are saying. The owner will have both keys.

### Example:

User A sends data to User B
A encrypts it so no one else can read it, just in case anyone sniffs out the packets
B will have the key to decrypt the encrypted message
The key must be provided by A in the first place

As the example mentions, that’s how Encrypt works. Every time you guys want to establish a secure communication, ENCRYPT it.

### Conclusion
Encrypt and HASH are not rivals, understand the usage and use them properly. Hash is not reversible, and Encrypt can ONLY be decrypted by trusted parties. Implementing these security gates in our apps will make them safe and secure.

title: Comments
excerpt: Comments are one of the core features that need to be in our source code. Before we start, I would like to say that `Comments` is a subjective topic, so other people might...
markdown:
Comments are one of the core features that need to be in our source code. Before we start, I would like to say that `Comments` is a subjective topic, so other people might have a different opinion.

So let’s dive in, comments are really useful and important when we do coding. There are thousands of benefits.

Here are 3 points that I would like to point out:
* After a month, when you see your code again, you will appreciate them.
* Your colleagues won’t scold you.
* You will be proud of yourself when you see the code after a year.

When should we use comments? Put your comments as info or on a function that has complex logic, or put a comment when you want to do something else in the future (a TODO comment). Remember, the best code is readable code. Don’t put 10 lines of comments for 5 lines of code. Always use a suitable name for a variable/function.

**Bad Example:**
// set x to 0
var x = 0
*This is a super bad comment; even my grandma knows that we are assigning x to 0.

**Good Example:**
// don't use the global isFinite() because it returns true for null values
Number.isFinite(value)
*This is a good comment because it is an informational comment and will help other colleagues.

Above are 2 simple examples that I found best to describe the difference between good and bad comments. Now I wanna share with you guys my convention for commenting. I have 3 types of comments (TODO:, **, >). Let’s go through them 1 by 1.

I use `// TODO: < comments />` when I want to do something in the future; I will write down the comment so next time I visit it, I will know. Before I deploy, I will do a global search for this keyword “TODO:”, check each one, make sure the doable tasks within the timeframe are done, and then delete the comment.

Next is `// ** `. This comment I use if I want to delete something. Sometimes we don’t know if we touch it whether it’s gonna blow up or stay in peace, so I play it safe by commenting it out first. Same as the previous step with a slight change—I will search for this keyword after I deploy. You know the reason why I do it this way.

Last is `// >`. This one I use to indicate a resource or links to a cool logic that I found on Stack Overflow / Medium / etc. Because that post or answer already explains it, I don’t want to repeat it in my comments, let's try our best to 'DRY'.

### Conclusion:
Comments must exist in our source code. Write good comments, and never ever write bad comments. Make your own or use my convention for commenting, and put the convention in your README file. This is my quick sharing about comments. Hope you guys got a good understanding of how comments work in source code.

title: README
excerpt: Today I will share about the *README* file, remember the Git post? Hope you guys remember it! A *README* file is an important core feature when you have a Git repo. The *README* file...
markdown:
Today I will share about the *README* file, remember the Git post? Hope you guys remember it! A *README* file is an important core feature when you have a Git repo. The *README* file is the file that will explain everything about your repo.

What is a *README* file? It is a file that contains an introduction, usage instructions, and a setup guide for your repo. These 3 notes are very important for the community. A *README* file is really easy to write, and people will appreciate it when they search for your repo.

A *README* file is written in 'md' format, and the extension will be 'README.md'. MD stands for **Markdown**, it is a lightweight markup language. It's super easy to learn and write. A good programmer will include a *README* file for every repo. That means when your repo has a good *README* file, then you are a good programmer.

What features does **Markdown (MD)** have? Since the *README* file is written in md format, let’s talk about its features. MD files can include images, hyperlinks, headers (h1, h2, … , h6), tables, lists, inline HTML, and a few more. It is super cool and will make your *README* file super interactive and easily understandable.

Conclusion: A *README* file is very important for every repo, highly customizable, and will make your repo outstanding. So, there is no reason for you not to write a *README* file. Start your *README* file today!

title: Postman
excerpt: Hi programmers! Today’s topic is **Postman**. I believe almost all of you guys know a postman—the guy who delivers our letters... haha, nope! I am not talking about the real postman...
markdown:
Hi programmers! Today’s topic is **Postman**. I believe almost all of you guys know a postman—the guy who delivers our letters... haha, nope! I am not talking about the real postman, I am talking about the 'Postman app'. It is one of those awesome apps that help us test our APIs.

**Postman** is a tool that we use to test our APIs, and it makes our work really, really easy. Here are a few points that I would like to share:

1. It features team collaboration.

2. You can create a collection of APIs.

3. It offers one-click documentation.

4. The documentation can be published to the world wide web for free.

5. One click, and the collection will be right there in your Postman.

These 5 points are more than enough to convince us to use it, especially point number 3. For the person who does the API documentation, they know how much of a pain in the ass it can be. Postman makes our life super easy because we can generate the API documentation with just one click.

The documentation is super clean and very easy to understand. At the bottom of this post, I will provide a link to an API documentation that was done via Postman. There are paid and free versions—of course, the paid version is much better—but here is the catch: the free version itself is already quite good!

Let’s talk about the publishing part. Once you publish the API doc, the client or tester can visit the documentation, and there is a button that says 'Run in Postman'. Know what that means? You are right—with one click, the whole collection of the APIs will be exported straight to their Postman. This is a super crazy feature! I think that's enough praising Postman; you guys should just give it a shot.

Conclusion: Postman can save tons of time—it is just like Git. There is no need to spend infinite time rewriting documentation. All the major parts are taken care of; we just need to add a description and a few screenshots. Your clients or the consumers of your API will praise you because they can export the whole collection with just one click.
